meta.title: Software That Writes Its Own Manual
meta.author: Tech Talk

hook.title: Software That Writes Its Own Manual
hook.subtitle: Recursion, dogfooding, and the art of building tools with themselves

the-problem.title: The Dumbest Thing We Keep Doing
the-problem.body:
We build software to automate tedious work. Then we manually maintain, document, and improve that software. The tool never uses itself.

This talk is about closing that loop — and why the simplest recursive strategy is almost always available but rarely taken.
bullets: Generators that build docs never read their own docs
AI assistants that write prompts aren't prompted with their own output
Deck generators that create presentations don't present to themselves

who-are-you.title: Before we go further — where do you live?
who-are-you.subtitle: Self-improvement looks different from different vantage points

engineer-lens.title: The Engineer's Frame: Feedback Loops
engineer-lens.body:
In systems thinking, a positive feedback loop either accelerates improvement or accelerates decay. Self-improving software routes its own outputs back as inputs.

The key insight: you don't need a second system to evaluate the first. The same interface that consumes output can generate the next version of it.
bullets: A linter that uses its own rules to write its config
A prompt template system that generates its own few-shot examples
A CLI tool that generates its own help text from its own usage patterns

product-lens.title: The Product Frame: Dogfooding at Scale
product-lens.body:
Dogfooding is eating your own dog food — using the product you build. But strategic dogfooding means routing your own usage data back into the development process.

Most teams dogfood reactively. Self-improving systems dogfood structurally — usage patterns automatically become inputs to the next generation.
bullets: Analytics that reshapes the dashboard based on navigation patterns
A content tool that learns your style and pre-populates templates
A deck generator that uses generated decks to improve generation

philosopher-lens.title: The Deep Frame: Autopoiesis
philosopher-lens.body:
Biologist Humberto Maturana coined autopoiesis — systems that produce the very components that constitute them. A cell makes the molecules that make the cell.

Software autopoiesis is when the system's outputs become inputs to its own specification. It's not magic — it's a design choice about where the loop closes.
bullets: The map that updates itself from the territory it describes
The compiler originally written in the language it now compiles
The AI assistant whose behavior is shaped by its own prior outputs

core-insight.title: The Core Insight: Close the Loop
core-insight.body:
Every self-improving system does one thing: it routes output back as input. The sophistication varies. The structure doesn't.

The simplest strategy is also the most powerful: use the tool to improve the tool. Not a second evaluation system. Not a separate training pipeline. The same interface, pointed at itself.
core-insight.code: // The pattern, distilled const improved = await tool.run(tool.describe()) tool.update(improved) // repeat until stable

deck-example.title: This Deck Was Built With Itself
deck-example.body:
The deck generator is a CLI that calls Claude to generate presentation JSON. The system prompt that shapes its behavior? Refined by running the tool on itself and observing the results.

When we add a new slide type, we generate a deck about the new slide type to test it. The tool eats its own output. Bugs surface immediately because the author is also the audience.
bullets: Generate deck → notice rough edges → refine the prompt → regenerate
Error messages in the CLI were written by asking the CLI to explain its own errors
The format spec in CLAUDE.md was sharpened by running bad generations through the validator

go-deeper.title: What do you want to take home?
go-deeper.subtitle: Pick the thread that matters most to you right now

branches.label: Why this is hard
branches.desc: The failure modes and traps to avoid

implementation.title: Starting Points That Actually Work
implementation.body: You don't start with full recursion. You start with one feedback edge — one place where the system's output informs its own next run.
bullets: Step 1: Identify what your tool produces that it doesn't currently consume
Step 2: Route the least-structured output back as structured input
Step 3: Let one iteration run — observe what changes
Step 4: Decide if the loop stabilizes or diverges, then tune
implementation.code: # deck generate → refine → regenerate deck generate README.md > deck.json deck refine deck.json "make it sharper" deck serve deck.json # eat your own output

failure-modes.title: The Traps
failure-modes.body: Self-improving systems can improve toward the wrong goal. The most common failure: optimizing for the feedback signal rather than the underlying value you actually care about.
bullets: Overfitting to your own patterns — the tool gets great at what you already do, nothing more
Runaway loops — each iteration drifts further from ground truth with no anchor
Loss of legibility — the evolved version is better but nobody understands why
Mirror chambers — the system reinforces its own assumptions with no external signal

future-directions.title: Where This Gets Interesting
future-directions.body: Single-tool recursion is the beginning. The richer pattern: ecosystems of tools that improve each other. Each tool's output becomes another tool's training signal.
bullets: Memory systems that track patterns across sessions and reshape how Claude opens new ones
Deck generators that learn from audience analytics to refine default slide structures
CI systems that use test failure signatures to generate better test scaffolding automatically

principles.title: Three Laws of Self-Improving Systems
principles.body: These three constraints separate systems that genuinely improve from systems that just iterate in circles.
bullets: 1. Output format must be a valid input format — or the loop can never close
2. The system must have a way to evaluate its own outputs — even a weak signal beats none
3. Each iteration must be observable — invisible improvement is indistinguishable from drift

closing.title: Build the Shovel With the Shovel
closing.subtitle: The simplest recursive strategy is always available. Most teams just never take it.
